does: This paper introduces CANARY, a zero-label detection method that identifies fine-tuning contamination in language models by analyzing hidden-state geometry rather than generated outputs. The approach works by taking two forward passes over unlabeled prompts and projecting hidden-state differences through a Sparse Autoencoder to isolate semantic drift while filtering out style noise. CANARY achieves perfect detection (AUROC = 1.000) at just 1% contamination levels—7.5 times lower than output-level defenses can detect—across multiple model architectures and training paradigms, with zero false positives on benign fine-tuning and robustness against adaptive attacks including style-matching and gradient-noise perturbations.
connects: This work builds on recent discoveries that poisoning attacks can implant dormant harmful behaviors that remain hidden in model internals despite being undetectable in model outputs. It extends prior research on understanding model hidden states and interpretability tools like Sparse Autoencoders, which have shown promise for decomposing neural activations into interpretable features. The paper responds to limitations of output-level defenses that can only detect contamination once harmful behavior manifests in generated text, combining insights from adversarial ML, model internals research, and mechanistic interpretability to develop a more sensitive detection approach.
enables: This detection method enables a more robust governance pipeline for fine-tuned models by exposing poisoning attempts before they manifest behaviorally. The SAE feature basis used for detection can also drive amplification techniques to surface latent harms at higher rates, creating opportunities for deeper auditing of model internals. The approach points toward interpretability-based security as a general strategy for detecting model tampering, and suggests that understanding hidden-state geometry could become fundamental to model validation and safety certification processes before deployment or wide release.
